coredata.dc:title: A fluorescent carbon dots synthesized at room temperature for automatic determination of nitrite in Sichuan pickles
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Guo-Qi
Shi, Yu-Han
Wu, Wei
Zhao, Yang
Xu, Zhi-Hong
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, highly fluorescent carbon dots were synthesized from sodium ascorbate and polyethyleneimine at room temperature (R-CDs). The proposed green synthesis method was energy-saving, environmentally friendly and easy online. R-CDs exhibit an optimal emission peak of 490 nm under excitation at 380 nm with a quantum yield of 32 %. R-CDs morphology, composition, and properties were characterized using TEM, FTIR, XPS, UV-vis and fluorescence spectroscopy. The study revealed that nitrite quenched the fluorescence of R-CDs under acidic conditions. Subsequently, this discovered reaction of R-CDs and nitrite was combined with flow-injection technology, and a simple, precise and automatic fluorescence strategy for nitrite determination was accomplished. The response to nitrite was linear in 5–300 μg·L−1 concentration range and the limit of detection was 2.85 μg·L−1 (3.3 S/k). This method was applied to nitrite determination in Sichuan pickles during the pickling process and results were consistent with the standard method, demonstrating its feasibility in practical applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Room temperature
Carbon dots
Nitrite
Fluorescence
Flow injection
Sichuan pickles

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of Sichuan pickles and food-sample pretreatment
Optimization of assay conditions for FI-FL-NO2 − system
Determination of nitrite in real samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We acknowledge the financial support from XiHua University Talent Introduction Program ( RX2200001988 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FI-FL-NO2 − manifold for the determination of nitrite. P: pump; S1, S2: sample loop; R1, R2: reagent loop; F: fluorophotometric detector. W1,2,3: waste; RC: reaction coil.

Characterization of R-CDs. (A) TEM image of R-CDs and their corresponding size distribution (inset); (B) fluorescence emission spectra of R-CDs; (C) the FTIR spectrum of R-CDs; (D) XPS survey spectrum of R-CDs.
The potential formation mechanism of R-CDs. (A) The oxidation and ring opening of AsA-Na form 2,3-diketogulonic acid. (B) Schiff base reaction of AsA-Na and PEI600.

Quenching mechanism of R-CDs fluorescence sensor for nitrite. (A) Fluorescence emission spectra of R-CDs with addition of various concentrations of NO2 – (from top to bottom: 0, 10, 20, 50, 80, 120, 150, 200, and 250 μg·L−1). (B) UV–vis absorption spectra of R-CDs and in the presence of NO2 –. (C) FT-IR spectra of R-CDs and in the presence of NO2 –. (D) fuorescence lifetime of R-CDs and in the presence of NO2 –. The concentrations of NO2 – was 200 μg·L−1.

Evaluation of FI-FL-NO2 − system performance. Recording curves obtained by repeating the experiment (left side); The right side shows the response signal of various concentrations of nitrate. Linear correlation curve of nitrate (inset).
The proposed method for the determination of nitrite in Sichuan pickles. (A) Test results of during the day; (B) Test results of day-to-day.
Schematic illustration for FI-FL-NO2 − detection of nitrite in Sichuan pickle vegetable using R-CDs.
Comparison of fluorometry determination of nitrite based on CDs.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: CA: citric acid; TLE: triethylenetetramine; p-PDA: p-phenylenediamine; PEG: polyethylene glycol; EDA: ethanediamine.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison of the results for nitrite determination using our proposed method and Griess-Saltzman method.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: *: Diluted 100 times used for detection; **: Diluted 5 times used for detection.
a: Proposed methods: ΔF = 0.51C nitrite + 20.1; b: Griess-Saltzman method: A = 0.05C nitrite + 0.01.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Recovery results of nitrite in Sichuan pickles obtained by proposed method.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: *The concentration values of samples and standards are after mixing by 1 + 1 ratio.
a. ΔF = 0.64C nitrite + 30.6; b. ΔF = 0.57 C nitrite + 24.9.

head.ce:title: A fluorescent carbon dots synthesized at room temperature for automatic determination of nitrite in Sichuan pickles

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemisty, School of Science, Xihua University, Chengdu 610039, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemisty, School of Science, Xihua University, Chengdu 610039, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, Sichuan University, Chengdu 610065, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Engineering, Sichuan University, Chengdu 610065, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The College of Environmental Science and Engineering, Nankai University, Tianjin 300350, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The College of Environmental Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: The College of Environmental Science and Engineering, Nankai University, Tianjin 300350, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shenzhen Changlong Technology Co Ltd., Longgang District, Shenzhen 518117, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shenzhen Changlong Technology Co Ltd., Longgang District, Shenzhen 518117, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Department of Chemisty, School of Science, Xihua University, Chengdu 610039, PR China (G.Q. Zhang).

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, highly fluorescent carbon dots were synthesized from sodium ascorbate and polyethyleneimine at room temperature (R-CDs). The proposed green synthesis method was energy-saving, environmentally friendly and easy online. R-CDs exhibit an optimal emission peak of 490 nm under excitation at 380 nm with a quantum yield of 32 %. R-CDs morphology, composition, and properties were characterized using TEM, FTIR, XPS, UV-vis and fluorescence spectroscopy. The study revealed that nitrite quenched the fluorescence of R-CDs under acidic conditions. Subsequently, this discovered reaction of R-CDs and nitrite was combined with flow-injection technology, and a simple, precise and automatic fluorescence strategy for nitrite determination was accomplished. The response to nitrite was linear in 5–300 μg·L−1 concentration range and the limit of detection was 2.85 μg·L−1 (3.3 S/k). This method was applied to nitrite determination in Sichuan pickles during the pickling process and results were consistent with the standard method, demonstrating its feasibility in practical applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: It is well known that nitrate in vegetables are continuously converted to nitrite owing to microbial metabolism and nitrosoreductase catalysis during the production and storage of Sichuan pickles [1]. However, excessive accumulation of nitrite in the human body, which when combined with amides or amines, forms carcinogenic strong N-nitrosamine compounds [2,3]. Sichuan pickles is a very popular accompaniment dish in Sichuan; therefore, it is necessary to determine nitrite content in such foods.

Reports have described analytical technologies for nitrite determination in food, which mainly involve spectrophotometry [4–7], chemiluminescence [8,9], Raman spectroscopy [10,11], electrochemical [12,13], and ion chromatography [14,15]. However, these methodologies suffer from limitations including complicated operations, expensive equipment, and time-consuming, which hinders their wide application [16]. Simple, sensitive and rapid fluorescence assays have attracted considerable attention [17]. Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) possess outstanding characteristics such as low toxicity, easy preparation, and excellent and tunable luminescence property [18]. Recent advances in CDs applications include light-emission devices, bio-imaging, sensor, catalysis, and functional materials [19].

It is worth noting that CDs has become a research hotspot as fluorescent probes for nitrite detection. A popular concept employs high temperature/pressure to process the raw materials and obtain various CDs, which can be used for fluorescent sensing of nitrite. Furthermore, these sensors have been applied to various sample matrices such as water [20,21], human serum [22], and cell imaging [3]. Based on this similar high temperature/pressure synthetic route, reports have shown that the combination of CDs and other probes can be effective in nitrite detection. A dual-emission fluorescent probe at 460 nm and 572 nm was prepared via organosilane reaction of CDs, silicon dots, and rhodamine B [23]. This study found that nitrite increased the fluorescence efficiency of KBrO3 quenching of CDs, which allowed the determination of nitrite in beef and vegetables. Additionally, using the principle that nitrite oxidizes Fe(II) to Fe(III) under acidic conditions, Fe(III)-sensitive CDs were introduced in order to quantify nitrite in sausage [24]. Hu et al. reported that nitrite altered the color of neutral red dye, where it inhibits the ability of neutral red to quench the fluorescence of CDs. This approach was further explored where a fluorometric and colorimetric dual-mode for nitrite detection in ham was examined [25]. Furthermore, our group introduced CDs into fluorescent capillary analysis creating a new micro-fluorescence detection technology, which allowed the determination of trace-levels of nitrite in ham [26].

The dehydration of the raw materials can produce nano-scale carbon-based materials; hence, various fluorescence properties of CDs can be achieved via the dehydration process. Previous studies have focused on high-temperature strategy for dehydration to achieve CDs preparation, which requires high energy consumption and environmental concerns. Therefore, the development of a new simple and energy-saving CDs preparation route, such as low temperature or room temperaturer preparation, is of great significance. For example, it has been reported that ascorbic acid (AsA) and Cu(Ac)2·H2O were mixed at 90 ℃ to obtain new CDs, which possess pH sensing, excitation and up-conversion fluorescence characteristics [27]. The reaction of monosaccharides [28] and acetylpyridinium chloride [29] with alkali at room temperature produces two kinds of green luminescent CDs. Recently, AsA and diethylenetriamine were employed as raw materials to prepare CDs at room temperature and their application as a fluorescent probe for determination and intracellular imaging of ferric ion [30]. Unfortunately, the examination of CDs preparation at room temperature for nitrite detection has yet to be reported.

In addition, due to the high sensitivity of the fluorescence method, it requires high accuracy of manual operations, especially in trace analysis. Flow injection analysis (FIA) proposed under the non-equilibrium state, provides this technology with the characteristics of automation, easy control and high precision [31–36]. Therefore, FIA coupled fluorescence analysis has very broad application prospects. Additionally, our group has attempted to combine CDs with FIA technology and the results were encouraging [37].

All chemicals used in the study were of analytical reagent grade. Ascorbic acid (AsA), sodium ascorbate (AsA-Na), and calcium ascorbate (AsA-Ca) were purchased from Ye-Yuan Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). NaHCO3, Na2CO3, Na2HPO4, NaH2PO4, Na2SO4, NaNO2, NaNO3, Na2S, NaBr, NaF, NaCl, FeCl3, FeCl2, Cu(NO3)2, MgSO4, NaBH4, and HCl were purchased from Ke-Long Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Chendu, China). PEI600, PEI1800, PEI10000, and PEI17000 were purchased from Aladdin Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). p-Aminobenzene sulfonic acid and N-ethylenediamine were obtained from Ron Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Fresh vegetables grown locally, such as white radish, red radish, carrot and cucumber, were purchased from the local supermarkets.

The proposed FIA system for nitrite determination included 3100-type flow injection processor (Jitian, Beijing) and F96s-type fluorophotometer (Lengguang, China), in which PVC tubing with inner diameters of 1.0 mm/0.5 mm and 0.8 mm PTFE pipelines were used to connect the manifold. The absorption spectra were obtained using UV-1800 spectrophotometer (MeiPuDa, China). TEM images were obtained using a Tecnai G2 F20 transmission electron microscope (FEI, USA). XPS was carried using K-Alpha (Thermo Scientific, USA). FT-IR analysis was conducted using a Spectrum-two-Li10014-type FTIR (Perkin Elmer, USA). Ultra-pure water (conductivity: 0.065 μS cm−1) was produced using AKL-UP-IV-10-type water purification system (Kangning, China), and it was used to prepare all solutions in the study.

R-CDs were prepared as follows: 0.24 g AsA-Na was placed into 50 mL conical flask containing 20 mL pure water. After it was completely dissolved, 20 mL PEI600 (w%=1 %) was added. Then, the mixture was stirred for 24 h, during which the solution turned from initial colorless to yellowish and finally to yellow. The obtained solution was dialyzed against ultrapure water through a dialysis bag (MWCO: 500) for 36 h to remove unreacted species. Finally, R-CDs inside the dialysis bag were collected and stored in a refrigerator. A portion of R-CDs solution was freeze-dried, which generated powder samples that were dry storage for further characterization.

The asynchronous-alternating merging-zone flow injection has the advantages of reducing reagent and sample size, as well as fast sample processing compared with the general flow injection system. A schematic diagram of the proposed flow injection fluorescence analysis system for nitrite (FI-FL-NO2 −) determination was shown in Fig. 1 . FI-FL-NO2 − system consisted of a peristaltic pump, two-position valve, reaction coil, fluorophotometer, flow-through holder, and PTFE pipeline with an inner diameter of 5 mm. The procedures for nitrite determination were as follows:
The two-position valve was in “Position I”, S1 and R1 were respectively filled with nitrite and R-CDs. Simultaneously, HCl carriers flow through S2, R2, reaction coil (RC), and fluorophotometer detector. A blank baseline was obtained from FI-FL-NO2 − system.
When the auto valve was switched to “Position II”, nitrite (from S1) and R-CDs (from R1) were simultaneously injected into HCl flow path. The nitrite sample and R-CDs reagent zones were pushed into RC, forming the fluorescence quenching products. Furthermore, the fluorescence signal was recorded by the fluorophotometer detector. Meanwhile, nitrite and R-CDs were loaded into S2 and R2 again for the next assay to improve analysis efficiency.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of Sichuan pickles and food-sample pretreatment
ce:section.ce:para: The fresh vegetables were cut into small pieces, then placed into a clean glass bottle containing salt and rock sugar. Finally, the glass bottle was completely sealed and placed in a dry and cool place at room temperature.

All the above food-samples were processed using the referenced method [38]. The vegetables were removed from the pickle jar, dried, chopped, mixed, and homogenized in sequence. 5 g homogenized sample was transferred to an Erlenmeyer flask with 50 mL H2O, and 1 mL NaOH (1 mol·L−1) solution was added. Next, the mixture was sonicated for 30 min, and shaken every 5 min to ensure the solid phase was completely dispersed. Afterwards, the mixture solution was diluted with pure water using 100 mL volumetric flask. Finally, the mixture was filtered using 0.45 μm filter paper and centrifuged for 15 min at 10,000 rpm. The solution was injected into FI-FL-NO2 − system described in section 2.4.

The experimental results indicated that the fluorescence intensity of R-CDs was closely related to the concentration of AsA-Na and PEI, as well as the molecular weight of PEI and the reaction time. Thus, the fluorescence intensity of R-CDs was evaluated to optimize the preparation procedure, where the obtained R-CDs was diluted to 0.1 % to eliminate the self-quenching effect (Fig. S1). The optimal conditions for R-CDs production were as follows: molecular mass of PEI was 600, reaction time was 24 h, and the concentrations of PEI and AsA-Na was 1 % and 0.6 M, respectively.

The generation of R-CDs were investigated by the TEM imaging and fluorescence spectroscopy. The results showed that R-CDs were monodisperse spherical nanoparticles (Fig. 2 A) with a size distribution of 1.3–13.0 nm (Fig. 2A, insert). As shown in Fig. 3 b, the fluorescence emission curves of R-CDs were obtained with a variety of excitation wavelengths (from 320 to 460 nm), and an obvious excitation-independent phenomenon with an emission maximum of 490 nm was observed under the maximum excitation wavelength of 380 nm. Additionally, when quinine sulfate was employed as a reference, the quantum yield of R-CDs at 490 nm was determined as 32 % (Table S1). The outstanding Stokes shift and satisfactory quantum yield make R-CDs a potential excellent fluorescence sensor. R-CDs functional groups were analyzed using FT-IR (Fig. 2C). The absorption peak at 1632 cm−1 related to the bending stretching vibration of CN, and the peak at 1037 cm−1 related to the stretching vibration of COC. Moreover, FT-IR spectra also showed a strong absorption peak at 3320 and 779 cm−1, corresponding to the stretching and bending stretching vibrations of NH. The results revealed that the above functional groups originated from raw materials of AsA-Na and PEI600, respectively. In addition, the surface elemental composition of R-CDs (Fig. 2D) was observed by XPS survey spectrum, which exhibited three typical peaks of C 1s, O 1s, and N 1s, with relative atomic percentages of 61.94 %, 13.30 %, and 24.76 %, respectively. C 1s spectrum (Fig. S2A) exhibited four peaks at 284.98, 285.58, 286.08, and 287.48 eV arising from CC/CC, CN, CO, and CO/CN, respectively. The high-resolution N 1s spectrum (Fig. S2B) showed three peaks at 398.88, 399.68, and 401.18 eV, corresponding to CN, NH, and NC, respectively. O 1s spectrum (see Fig. S2C) displayed two peaks at 531.28 and 532.18 eV, assigned to CO and CO, respectively. The above data were consistent with FT-IR analysis, and again confirmed the presence of CN functional groups.

In this study, R-CDs were prepared from PEI600 and AsA-Na at room temperature. The proposed formation mechanism of R-CDs was illustrated in Fig. 3. During the reaction process, AsA-Na was readily oxidized, promoting ring-opening and conversion to 2,3-diketogulonic acid, which contained many active sites such as COOH, CO, and OH groups. PEI600 has an abundance of amine groups that can under Schiff base or amidation reactions with the above intermediate products, followed by subsequent inter-molecular dehydration generating large molecular aggregates. Due to the minimization of surface energy, the aggregates collapse to form nano-scale fluorescent carbon material.

Our examination revealed the rationality for the potential formation mechanism of R-CDs through confirmation of the Schiff base reaction. Fig. S3 shows FT-IR spectra of the raw materials and R-CDs. Comparison of all spectra revealed a new absorption peak of R-CDs at 1632 cm−1, attributed to the characteristic functional groups of Schiff base products of CN group. Additionally, the characteristic absorption peak of CN found at 350 nm [39] was observed in the UV-vis absorption spectrum of R-CDs (Fig. S4).

However, reports have suggested that the reduction property of raw materials were a significant cause of fluorescent carbon material preparation [40–42]. In this study, a strong reducing agent (NaBH4) was used instead of AsA-Na to evaluate the reduction effect towards the raw materials in R-CDs preparation. The results are shown in Fig. S5. NaBH4/PEI product had no-fluorescence properties, indicating that the raw materials reducibility had no influence on R-CDs formation (AsA-Na/PEI600). In addition, AsA, AsA-Na, and AsA-Ca as raw materials were reacted with PEI to prepare R-CDs, respectively. As shown in Fig. S5, the fluorescence emission peaks of R-CDs were at approx. 490 nm, which indicates that PEI600 of R-CDs prepared from AsA and its salts was comparable. Furthermore, the fluorescence intensity relationship of these three R-CDs occurred due to Ca(II) causing fluorescence quenching of R-CDs and AsA-Na was easier to undergo ring-opening and bind to PEI than AsA.

The properties of R-CDs were investigated to facilitate the development of fluorescent probes, and photostability tests were carried out first (Fig. S6A). The fluorescent intensity of R-CDs attenuated and decreased slightly after continuous irradiation by sunlight, but this has no influence on its ability to act as a fluorescent probe, which indicates that R-CDs possesses outstanding photostability. Moreover, the properties of R-CDs under various application conditions (pH and temperature) was investigated. As shown in Fig. S6B, the relationship between temperature and fluorescence intensity of R-CDs was inversely proportional, this was consistent with the characteristics of fluorescent probes. Interestingly, R-CDs fluorescence intensity had a satisfactory linear relationship with temperature at the maximum emission wavelength of 490 nm (Fig. S6C). Furthermore, the fluorescence response of R-CDs at different pH values was examined, where R-CDs could obtain excellent reagent blanks for use as fluorescent probes under acidic conditions (pH = 0.5–2) (Fig. S6D). In a complex environment matrix, the sensing ability of fluorescent probes was affected by salt concentration. As shown in Fig. S6E, only when the concentration of NaCl exceeded 100 g·L−1 did the fluorescence intensity of R-CDs quench, thereby affecting the overall fluorescence performance. Furthermore, this characteristic allows R-CDs to be used as a fluorescent probe in high-salt concentration environments.

The as-prepared R-CDs were examined for their ability to detect nitrite using the fluorescence strategy (Fig. 4 A). As nitrite concentration increased from 0 to 250 μg·L−1, the fluorescence intensity of R-CDs was gradually decreased. In addition, the fluorescence quenching efficiency of R-CDs obeyed a linear relationship with the concentration of nitrite (Fig. 4A, insert). Therefore, R-CDs were considered potential fluorescent probes for nitrite detection. Next, the possible reason for the quenching R-CDs probes towards nitrite was examined via the diazotization mechanism (see Fig. 4B inset). It is well known that nitrite is protonated to form nitrosonium ([ON]+) under acidic conditions [26,43]. Based on the diazo coupling reaction, a new N-nitroamine was generated by the nitrosonium and R-CDs, which contained an abundance of amino groups from PEI600 raw material. Electron transfer occurred between R-CDs and nitrosonium during the diazotization process, accompanied by fluorescence quenching of R-CDs.

To further verify this theory, UV–vis absorption spectra and infrared adsorption spectra of R-CDs in the presence of nitrite were studied. As shown in Fig. 4B, the absorption spectra of R-CDs were changed by the addition of nitrite. And then, after the addition of nitrite, the absorption peak of FTIR spectra of R-CDs show significant changes. As shown in Fig. 4C, a new absorption peak was clearly observed at 1266 cm−1, corresponding to the new functional group of the stretching vibrations of NN. Excitingly, evidence supports the proposed mechanism of diazotization. Finally, Effect of nitrite on the fluorescence lifetime of R-CDs was investigated (see Fig. 4D), the lifetime of R-CDs gradually decrease in 0 (5.93 ns) and 100 μg·L−1 (4.11 ns) NO2 −, which suggested the fuorescence dynamic quenching process. The original experimental results were shown in Fig. S7.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of assay conditions for FI-FL-NO2 − system
ce:section.ce:para: The conditions of FI-FL-NO2 − system were optimized by examining a variety of factors that may affect the method (Fig. S8). According to the reported results [35], the initial FI-FL-NO2 − system conditions were as follows: volume of S1 and S2 were 80 μL; RC was 250 cm and the total flow rate was 2 mL min−1, volume of R1 and R2 were 30 μL, length of LSR and LRR was 12 and 15 cm, respectively. HCl (sample solvent) and R-CDs filled with the sample and reagent loop, respectively, were injected into the FI-FL-NO2 − system, and the fluorescence blank value was recorded as Fo. Other fluorescence (F) intensities obtained by injecting the nitrite sample solution, allowed the calculation ΔF (Fo - F) to optimize the experimental conditions or obtain work curve of nitrite.

Adhering to the principle of linearity and sensitivity, the optimized FI-FL-NO2 − system parameters were summarized as follows: the samples volume (S1 and S2) was 150 μL; the reaction coil was 180 cm; pH of HCl carrier was 0.5; the volume of reagent (R1 and R2) was 100 μL; R-CDs concentration was 0.2 mg·L−1; the temperature was 20 ℃.

Under optimized conditions, the precision and linear range of the FI-FL-NO2 − system was investigated to evaluate its performance. In brief, the standard solutions of nitrite (3 μg·L−1 and 10 μg·L−1) were repeatedly injected into the FI-FL-NO2 − system (n = 11) and their fluorescence intensity value was recorded, respectively. The collected curves are shown in Fig. 5 , where the relative standard deviation (RSD) of the system was below 1.62 % through calculation.

To investigate the linear range for nitrite determination, the various concentrations of nitrite standard solutions were tested (Fig. 5, right side). The linear regression equation was as follows: ΔF = 0.51C nitrite + 20.1 (r = 0.996) within the nitrite concentration range of 5–300 μg·L−1, based on 3.3 S/k rule (S = 0.44, k = 0.51), the detection limit of the method was 2.85 μg·L−1. Table 1 compares the proposed method for nitrite determination with published ones. The optimized FI-FL-NO2 − system shows the sensitivity and range of linearity was more suitable for nitrite quantification in Sichuan pickle and food safety applications.

Under optimized conditions, the selectivity of presented method was investigated by observing the effects of different ions at a concentration ratio of 1 + 1. Certain potential coexisting ions, including HCO3 –, SO4 2−, CO3 2–, S2−, F−, Cl−, Br−, I−, NO3 –, H2PO4 −, HPO4 2−, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Al3+, Na+, were used as test samples or its were mixtured with standard solution of nitrite, and were respectively injected into the optimized FI-FL-NO2 – system. Fig. S9 clearly shows that effect of other ions on nitrite quenching R-CDs fluorescence was negligible. The results demonstrate the high anti-interference ability of FI-FL-NO2 − strategy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of nitrite in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Sichuan pickles, also called bathe pickles, can be eaten after soaking for one or two nights in salt water, and is a popular accompaniment dish in Sichuan. In this study, fresh vegetables such as white radish, radish, carrot and cucumber were selected as pickled vegetable samples, to verify the applicability of the proposed method for nitrite determination.

First, the determination of nitrite in vegetables was carried out using the proposed method and standard method, namely the Griess-Saltzman method (Fig. S10). The obtained results listed in Table 2 were comparable to that of the standard method. This showed that the present method was accurate and reliable.

Finally, the proposed method was applied to nitrite determination during the pickling process of Sichuan pickles. The results are shown in Fig. 6 . During the curing process, the nitrite content increased and then decreased. Obviously, this adheres to the confines of a healthy diet. Due to the regulations of the World Health Organization and the Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations, the daily allowable amount of nitrite for adults is 7.8 mg. Therefore, this further illustrates the potential application of this established method in the food industry. In addition, in order to verify the accuracy of the proposed FI-FL-NO2 − system, four real samples were spiked with nitrite standards for recovery experiments. The data obtained was listed and counted into Table 3 , the recovery data was in the range of 96.68–104.79 %, indicating that the presented method were accurate and reliable.

In conclusion, a fluorescent R-CDs probe was synthesized at room temperature, which conforms to the principles of green chemistry. The proposed formation mechanism of R-CDs involved inter-molecular dehydration, which was triggered by the Schiff base reaction of the raw materials. The prepared R-CDs combined with FI-FL-NO2 − technology could serve as a sensitive and selective sensor for nitrite detection. In addition, this strategy was successfully applied for nitrite detection in Sichuan pickles. The obtained results and those reported for standard methods for nitrite determination were similar, hence, the proposed methodology and concepts could be extended to the application fields of room-temperature-derived CDs.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We acknowledge the financial support from XiHua University Talent Introduction Program (RX2200001988).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2022.122025.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: p. Nitrate in vegetables: toxicity, content, intake and EC regulation J. Sci. Food Agric. 86 (2006). doi:10 17 10.1002/jsfa.2351.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Conditions for acid catalysed luminal nitrosation are maximal at the gastric cardia

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescent recognition and selective detection of nitrite ions with carbon quantum dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Li WH, Huang SP, Wen HY, Luo YN, Cheng JW, Jia Z, Han P, Xue WM. Fluorescent recognition and selective detection of nitrite ions with carbon quantum dots. Anal Bioanal Chem. 2020;412:993-1002. https://doi.org/10.1007/s00216-019-02325-9.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A facile dual-function fluorescent probe for detection of phosgene and nitrite and its applications in portable chemosensor analysis and food analysis

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Yang L, Wang F, Zhao J, Kong XJ, Lu K, Yang M, Zhang J, Sun ZW, You JM. Talanta 2021;221:121477. https://10.1016/j.talanta.2020.121477.
